content.problemStatement:
You have two arrays A=A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA = A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA=A1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã and B=B1,B2,‚Ä¶,BNB = B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_NB=B1‚Äã,B2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,BN‚Äã. Each of these elements also has a color associated with them, which is an integer. This is denoted by the arrays ColorA1,ColorA2,‚Ä¶,ColorANColorA_1, ColorA_2, \ldots, ColorA_NColorA1‚Äã,ColorA2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,ColorAN‚Äã, and ColorB1,ColorB2,‚Ä¶,ColorBNColorB_1, ColorB_2, \ldots, ColorB_NColorB1‚Äã,ColorB2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,ColorBN‚Äã.

In a single operation, you can swap any element of array AAA with any element of array BBB, if they have the same color.

Your goal is to do as many operations as you want, and eventually have the elements in array AAA be in non-decreasing order. That is, after you are done with the operations, you want A1‚â§A2‚â§‚Ä¶‚â§ANA_1 \leq A_2 \leq \ldots \leq A_NA1‚Äã‚â§A2‚Äã‚â§‚Ä¶‚â§AN‚Äã.

Output "Yes" if this is possible to do so, and "No" otherwise.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of five lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, denoting the size of the arrays.
The next line has NNN integers: A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã
The next line has NNN integers: ColorA1,ColorA2,‚Ä¶,ColorANColorA_1, ColorA_2, \ldots, ColorA_NColorA1‚Äã,ColorA2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,ColorAN‚Äã
The next line has NNN integers: B1,B2,‚Ä¶,BNB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_NB1‚Äã,B2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,BN‚Äã
The next line has NNN integers: ColorB1,ColorB2,‚Ä¶,ColorBNColorB_1, ColorB_2, \ldots, ColorB_NColorB1‚Äã,ColorB2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,ColorBN‚Äã




Output Format

For each testcase, in a new line, output "Yes" if it is possible to end up with the array AAA having elements in non-decreasing order. Else, print "No".
You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings YES, yEs, yes, and yeS will all be treated as identical).
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2‚ãÖ1052 \cdot 10^52‚ãÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Testcase 1: The given arrays are:

A=[3,2]A = [3, 2]A=[3,2]
ColorA=[1,2]ColorA = [1, 2]ColorA=[1,2]
B=[1,2]B = [1, 2]B=[1,2]
ColorB=[1,1]ColorB = [1, 1]ColorB=[1,1]

A1A_1A1‚Äã and B1B_1B1‚Äã have the same color (111). So, we can swap them, and now we have the arrays as:

A=[1,2]A = [1, 2]A=[1,2]
B=[3,2]B = [3, 2]B=[3,2]

Now, array AAA is sorted in non-decreasing order. So, it is possible to achieve this, and hence the answer is "Yes".
Testcase 2: The given arrays are:

A=[13]A = [13]A=[13]
ColorA=[1]ColorA = [1]ColorA=[1]
B=[23]B = [23]B=[23]
ColorB=[1]ColorB = [1]ColorB=[1]

We see that the array AAA is already sorted. Hence, the answer is "Yes".
Testcase 3: The given arrays are:

A=[2,1]A = [2, 1]A=[2,1]
ColorA=[2,1]ColorA = [2, 1]ColorA=[2,1]
B=[2,1]B = [2, 1]B=[2,1]
ColorB=[2,1]ColorB = [2, 1]ColorB=[2,1]

We see that no matter how many operations we do, the two arrays will remain the same, and AAA can never be sorted. Hence the answer is "No".
